## 2016-12-13

**Levi Waldron** (16:45:02):
> About me:
> * assistant professor at the CUNY School of Public Health in NYC
> * I teach introductory biostat and regression methods for our MPH program in epic/bios
> * Bioconductor user only since about 2007
> * education was in physics and wood science
> * involved in MultiAssayExperiment, curatedMetagenomicData, ovarian cancer subtype validation, validation of prediction models, and a few other things
> * cyclist, coffee drinker
> * still in denial about Trump

**Nitesh Turaga** (21:31:51):
> About me:
> - New addition to the Bioconductor Core team at Roswell Park.
> - Previously a Software Engineer at the Galaxy Project at Johns Hopkins.
> - Still figuring Buffalo, NY out. 
> - I like to travel, and Crossfit.


## 2016-12-15

**Phil Chapman** (08:55:42):
> About me:
> - At the University of Manchester in the UK working in the Drug Discovery Unit at the Cancer Research UK Manchester Institute
> - Previously at AstraZeneca
> - Bioconductor user since 2012
> - Originally a molecular biologist before moving into bioinformatics
> - Interested in applying published pharmacogenomic methods to data we have generated on our preclinical compounds, also RNAseq, methylation, lots of things really - whatever it takes to move our projects forward towards the clinic and find/validate new drug discovery targets
> - Like working with normalised/tidy data i.e. SQL db's, tidyverse etc
> - Kind of relieved Trump got elected as everyones stopped laughing at us about Brexit

**Sean Davis** (11:25:28):
> @Phil Chapman, have you played with BigQuery much? It is really amazing technology and is really ripe for data integration with pharmacogenomics data.  If you need an account and happen to be working on anything cancer-related, let me know.

**Phil Chapman** (11:40:08):
> Thanks@Sean Davisthat sounds really interesting, i've heard@Vince Careytalk about it but not tried it myself.  Have been mainly using@Benjamin Haibe-Kains's PharmacoGx recently as it stores drug response data as well as genomic data and solves a number of issues such as cell line naming conventions.

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (11:42:12):
> @Phil Chapmanthat reminds me to work on the port from`pSet`to`MultiAssayExperiment`..

## 2017-11-29

**Matthew McCall** (09:35:13):
> @Matthew McCall has joined the channel

**Matthew McCall** (09:41:25):
> Hello all. I’m an Assistant Professor in Biostatistics and Computational Biology at University of Rochester. I have several BioC packages and more in the works. I’m currently working a lot with network-like data for which it would be useful to store information about pairs (possibly groups) of features rather than single individual features. Not sure if something like this already exists in BioC.

**Ludwig Geistlinger** (09:47:34):
> Hi Matthew, the`graph`package implements general representations for networks. On the other hand, the`InteractionSet`package is a more specific package for genomic interaction data. In case, you are missing functionality for your data, this would be interesting to discuss on bioc devel.

**Sean Davis** (09:48:58):
> Hi,@Matthew McCall. Good to see you again.

**Matthew McCall** (09:54:27):
> Hi Sean! I recalled hearing about this slack at the last BioC but was too busy to follow up at the time.@Stephanie Hicksgets credit for providing me the link to join.

**Matthew McCall** (09:57:29):
> Hi Ludwig, we should probably move this conversation out of#introductionsbut I do use the graph package for some stuff. The limitation there is (to my knowledge) the inability to link the relational data to the marginal data (e.g. feature counts in a SummarizedExperiment). I wasn’t aware of the InteractionSet package. That looks closer to what I’m interested in, but I’ll need to dig more to see whether it would work.
  - Attachment: [Attachment](https://community-bioc.slack.com/archives/C36B0HK7W/p1511966854000354)
> Hi Matthew, the `graph` package implements general representations for networks. On the other hand, the `InteractionSet` package is a more specific package for genomic interaction data. In case, you are missing functionality for your data, this would be interesting to discuss on bioc devel.

**Levi Waldron** (09:59:05):
> Hi@Matthew McCall! There's been some related discussion in the#tree-like-sechannel here. Our application has been more specifically focused on microbiome data, but I have wondered whether the data class we're discussing should be more general.  Would be interested in your input.@natedolsonhas posted a draft class of an extension of DataFrame to represent phylogenetic trees, which are a network. The motivation for extending DataFrame is that it could then be used as rowData in a SummarizedExperiment.

**Matthew McCall** (10:05:28):
> Hi@Levi Waldron. Yes, that’s exactly what I was thinking — that this info could be a special type of rowData if formatted correctly. I just joined the#tree-like-sechannel, and I’ll take a look at the DataFrame extension for phylo trees. Thanks!

**Simina Boca** (14:04:26):
> Piggy-backing onto what@Matthew McCallsaid, if we have questions similar to his questions (e.g. "I would like this feature, but if it doesn't exist I would be happy to help out") what is the recommended decision tree of posting it here vs bioc-devel or elsewhere?

**Lucas Schiffer** (14:10:57):
> @Simina BocaI don't know that there is a consensus just yet but I see bioc-devel as more for something like "I am having a problem with X, can someone help me". Then Slack is more about envisioning new things and cooking up ideas like#tree-like-se:wink:

**Simina Boca** (14:12:08):
> Got it! Cool, thanks!

**Simina Boca** (14:14:03):
> Many of my applied projects involve metabolomics, so if there's a lot of interest in that, I would be happy to at least brainstorm some ideas around usability and some workflows in that space (I know at least@Kasper D. Hansenwork on some metabolomics too)

**Levi Waldron** (14:42:24) (in thread):
> The Slack channel does seem to be more interactive around this kind of cooking up of ideas. That said, I think it doesn't hurt to post that kind of question on bioc-devel as well, since I believe it has a larger membership.

**Levi Waldron** (14:44:39) (in thread):
> I don't have a specific interest around metabolomics, but as one of the authors of MultiAssayExperiment ([http://bioconductor.org/packages/MultiAssayExperiment/](http://bioconductor.org/packages/MultiAssayExperiment/)), I'm interested in seeing that how different kinds of data are represented and making sure they're compatible with MultiAssayExperiment!
  - Attachment (Bioconductor): [MultiAssayExperiment](http://bioconductor.org/packages/MultiAssayExperiment/)
> Multi-assay 'omics experiments on a set of samples are increasingly commonplace in biomedical research. MultiAssayExperiment implements data structures and methods for representing, manipulating, and integrating multi-assay experiments via efficient construction, subsetting, and extraction operations. These methods are implemented matching Bioconductor user experience by straightforward extending concept and design of single-assay classes such as SummarizedExperiment or ExpressionSet.

**Simina Boca** (15:51:47) (in thread):
> Cool! Let's keep in touch! You're also funded through ITCR right? I just became an ITCR investigator in June.

## 2018-12-18

**Lluís Revilla** (07:29:10):
> I'm the developer of[https://doi.org/doi:10.18129/B9.bioc.BioCor](https://doi.org/doi:10.18129/B9.bioc.BioCor), if you want to know more about it let me know
  - Attachment (Bioconductor): [BioCor](https://doi.org/doi:10.18129/B9.bioc.BioCor)
> Calculates functional similarities based on the pathways described on KEGG and REACTOME or in gene sets. These similarities can be calculated for pathways or gene sets, genes, or clusters and combined with other similarities. They can be used to improve networks, gene selection, testing relationships...


## 2018-12-19

**Leo Lahti** (04:37:00):
> @Leo Lahti has joined the channel

**Leo Lahti** (04:40:55):
> Hello Bioc. I just found my way in this Slack. Author of 4 bioc packages over the last 10 years, including the latest one, microbiome R package ([http://microbiome.github.io/microbiome/](http://microbiome.github.io/microbiome/)). I met some of you at the European BIoc Meeting in Munich this year. More info on our research at[https://openresearchlabs.github.io/](https://openresearchlabs.github.io/)- currently my primary interests include combinations of machine learning & ecological models and multi-omics data integration in the context of microbiome research.
  - Attachment (openresearchlabs.github.io): [Open Research Labs - Home](https://openresearchlabs.github.io/)
> Open Research Labs, University of Turku, Finland.

## 2019-04-25

**Jake Taylor-King** (06:28:06):
> @Jake Taylor-King has joined the channel

**Jake Taylor-King** (06:35:02):
> Hi all, I was hoping for some advice. I'm a bioinformatician typically working on RNA-Seq with patient data.
> 
> However, a new project has come up working with DNA-Seq on a novel plant variety. We need someone who can construct a library for the genome from scratch, vaguely estimate genes of interest through homology between species, and thereafter choose genes for KO experiments (with some end phenotype in mind). They would also be working with experienced plant biologists.
> 
> We're in the process of writing a job advert. If anyone has 3 minutes, could you let me know the kind of packages/programmes I would be expecting someone to have experience in?

**Jake Taylor-King** (06:38:35):
> Thanks in advance! Best, Jake

**Vince Carey** (14:46:43):
> @Jake Taylor-Kinghave you considered posting your question to[support.bioconductor.com](http://support.bioconductor.com)?  There is a much broader readership.  The slack might well be highly biased towards applications to human genome.

**Vince Carey** (14:58:58):
> On the other hand if you are talking about constructing Bioconductor packages (you mention "library for the genome" which can have various interpretations) it might be worthwhile to have a look at all the packages at[http://bioconductor.org/packages/release/BiocViews.html#___Arabidopsis_thaliana](http://bioconductor.org/packages/release/BiocViews.html#___Arabidopsis_thaliana)-- this addresses a variety of annotation approaches relevant for a plant.  The landscape of packages ranging from BSgenome* through TxDb* to hom* should be understood by anyone tackling the tasks you mention in a Bioc-oriented way.


## 2019-04-26

**Jake Taylor-King** (06:26:55):
> Hi@Vince Carey- thanks for the message, much appreciated.:slightly_smiling_face:

## 2019-05-24

**Pratima Chennuri** (13:13:54):
> @Pratima Chennuri has joined the channel

**Nicholas Knoblauch** (13:25:49):
> Hi everybody, I'm Nick Knoblauch, a  PhD student in the Genetics Genomics and Systems Biology program at the University of Chicago and a part-time consultant for the HDF group (the nonprofit behind`HDF5`).  I'm a long time`HDF5`and`R`user in my own research (statistical molecular genetics) but I'd be curious to know more about the broader`R`/`HDF5`ecosystem in the life sciences (e.g what do people like/not like?)

**Tim Triche** (15:44:24):
> people would like HDF5 and restfulSE / HSDS a lot if they knew more about it, I think@Nicholas Knoblauch:slightly_smiling_face:

**Tim Triche** (15:45:22):
> if you work with John Readey, please pass along my apologies for taking so damned long to get the TARGET multiAssayExperiments into a Restful form for HSDS.  He has been very helpful and I am remiss for falling behind

**Nicholas Knoblauch** (16:30:13):
> @Tim TricheI agree!  I think@John Readeyis around the channel from time to time. One thing I'm particularly interested in helping out with around here is HSDS, so if there's anything I can do to help let me know!

**Tim Triche** (16:31:21):
> no good deed goes unpunished... will be corresponding with you next week I think:slightly_smiling_face:


## 2019-05-27

**Justin** (15:53:45):
> @Justin has joined the channel

**Ni Zhao** (18:30:49):
> @Ni Zhao has joined the channel

**Vince Carey** (20:11:09):
> @Nicholas Knoblauchthere is a brief discussion of HSDS in relation to Bioconductor at[https://f1000research.com/articles/8-21/v1](https://f1000research.com/articles/8-21/v1)
  - Attachment (f1000research.com): [F1000Research Article: restfulSE: A semantically rich interface for cloud-scale genomics with Bioconductor.](https://f1000research.com/articles/8-21/v1)
> Read the latest article version by Shweta Gopaulakrishnan, Samuela Pollack, BJ Stubbs, Hervé Pagès, John Readey, Sean Davis, Levi Waldron, Martin Morgan, Vincent Carey, at F1000Research.

**Vince Carey** (20:13:20):
> @Tim Tricheonce your HDF5 for TARGET is in an S3 bucket there should be no problem getting it indexed in HSDS.  Let me know the situation.


## 2019-05-28

**Kirk Reardon** (02:53:17):
> @Kirk Reardon has joined the channel

**Tim Triche** (06:51:38):
> I know -- the deal with TARGET pAML is mostly that it has expanded so rapidly (@Vince Carey)

**Tim Triche** (06:54:44):
> @Vince Carey: you and John and now@Nicholas Knoblauchhave been absurdly patient and I thank you for that.  This week actually will be the end of the game, though; we're resubmitting to Leukemia, Blood, and eventually (garbled glam pub) so it's all I'm really doing these days. Also, thanks for the work on restfulSE -- I noticed that an enormous number of large-scale studies are in there now!  The TARGET pAML data is a huge MAE which will only get bigger with the WGS+WES (yes, both) data and DNA/RNA allelic imbalance results. I guess those are the only results that won't be going in this week.

## 2019-08-16

**Mikhael Manurung** (09:10:07):
> Greetings everyone! I'm a PhD student at Leiden University Medical Center, the Netherlands. I have only been working with R/BioC for a year. However, it has been such a transforming experience and I am delighted to found such a welcoming community:slightly_smiling_face:.

**Mikhael Manurung** (09:11:03) (in thread):
> Are you the one who made the vici package?

**Boris Hejblum** (09:11:29) (in thread):
> Hi Mikhael

**Boris Hejblum** (09:11:36) (in thread):
> Yes, I am.


## 2019-08-17

**Mikhael Manurung** (12:21:29) (in thread):
> Thanks for making the package! I have tried to ask a statistician in my department to do something about background-subtracting practice when working with intracellular-cytokine staining data (some also set negative values to zero!), but no one really cared about it.

**Boris Hejblum** (20:17:29) (in thread):
> Thanks, I’m glad you liked it ! A paper should be coming out soon. Any feedback on VICI is welcomed I added a few features last week


## 2019-08-18

**Luke Zappia** (19:34:26):
> @Luke Zappia has joined the channel


## 2019-08-19

**Mikhael Manurung** (05:22:40) (in thread):
> I look forward to the paper! If you don't mind, could you elaborate on why you use gls instead of lme for the intra-arm fit? If we use lme then I can use subject ID as random effect to take into account the repeated measures, right?

**Kin Lau** (17:42:26):
> @Kin Lau has joined the channel


## 2019-08-20

**Al J Abadi** (20:28:25):
> @Al J Abadi has joined the channel


## 2019-08-23

**Boris Hejblum** (06:06:55) (in thread):
> This is a good question ! nlme::gls is what lets us fit the bivariate model we propose (with distinct variances for background and signal). Adding a random effect to such a model could lead to numerical convergence issues when the number of individuals is not large enough (and in our phase I/II trials we often have only a couple dozen of patients). An additional reason no to is to facilitate interpretation of the results, in particular for our immunologist collaborators.

**Tim Thurman** (13:13:48):
> @Tim Thurman has joined the channel


## 2019-08-24

**Madison Tyler** (09:33:22):
> @Madison Tyler has joined the channel


## 2019-08-25

**Xiaotong Wang** (20:21:37):
> @Xiaotong Wang has joined the channel


## 2019-08-26

**Mikhael Manurung** (10:34:08) (in thread):
> That would be an issue indeed. However, what if we add information on the within-group correlation structure such as with`correlation = corCompSymm(, form = ~1|ID)`? In this way, we would avoid going into mixed models and decorrelate the residuals. What do you think?

**Boris Hejblum** (10:49:25) (in thread):
> Well I think this formula fits a different variance for each individual, which would requires that you have enough measured time-points. In our case we typically have only two which is not nearly enough to get stable estimates.

**Mikhael Manurung** (11:04:14) (in thread):
> Thanks for the prompt response. If one has enough time points, would the approach I describe previously be the better one?
> 
> By the way, could you point me to a reference about sample size/time point requirement to get stable estimates? Thanks again for sharing your insights.

**Boris Hejblum** (11:11:20) (in thread):
> It’s hard to talk of “the better” approach. Your proposed model would have more flexibility because it has more parameters, but the larger question would be whether this flexibility would be useful to answer your scientific question of interest. In the case of a large inter-individual variability it would probably be nice to to incorporate that in the model, as you propose to do.
> 
> Now concerning sample-size/time points requirements I’m not sure there is a precise answer. The minimum to be able to compute a variance is two points, so you definitely need more than that to get a reliable estimate...

> Hi all, I'm Giuseppe, a postcoctoral researcher in Sarah Langley's lab at LKC Medical School in Singapore. > Hello everyone, about me:
> -biologist turned bioinformatician long time ago

## 2020-04-30

**Chase Clark** (13:06:48):
> Hello, I'm grad student in Chicago and have written a couple of R packages ([chasemc.github.io/electricShine](http://chasemc.github.io/electricShine)) ([https://chasemc.github.io/IDBac/](https://chasemc.github.io/IDBac/)).
> In the not-too-distant future I'll be transitioning from mostly metabolomics to metagenomics/metatranscriptomics/genomics and would love suggestions of reading material for transitioning to python from R but also blogs/etc on current Bioc tools for those fields.

**Nicholas Knoblauch** (13:23:09):
> If you've already written R packages this may be a bit basic, but I usually point python programmers towards[https://tidynomicon.tech/](https://tidynomicon.tech/)
  - Attachment (tidynomicon.tech): [The Tidynomicon](https://tidynomicon.tech/)
> The Tidynomicon

**Chase Clark** (14:31:26) (in thread):
> Thanks, I'm actually looking for the opposite direction, python for someone who primarily programs in R

**Nicholas Knoblauch** (14:54:06) (in thread):
> oops.  As someone who does transcriptomics/genomics and uses R as my main scripting language I've found that unlike R, a lot of the bioinformatics tools written in python are meant to be run from the command line (the genomics community has a pretty intense flat text fetish) , and don't actually require much python knowledge.  That being said, snakemake is a pretty excellent workflow management tool that requires almost no python knowledge to start using (and is also very R friendly)

## 2020-05-03

**Nitin Sharma** (11:11:22):
> Hello everyone, Nitin here.
> 
> I have a PhD in structural bioinformatics from the National University of Singapore. I have done postdocs in Cambridge and then CRUK-MI, where I worked in the analysis of RNA-Seq data mainly, with some exposure to Methylation data and Single-Cell RNA-seq analysis. I am currently working as a postdoc at the Institute of Cancer Research, Oslo to understand the drug resistance and cancer evolution in the gastrointestinal stromal tumour (GIST) using RNA-Seq, microarray and whole-exome analysis and Single-cell analysis.
> 
> I will be more active in single cell domain from now on and have created a channel#singlecell-queriesfor more general queries regarding single-cell analysis.

**Shuyu Zheng** (09:59:29):
> Hi everyone,
> I’m a Ph.D. student in University of Helsinki. I’m working on drug combination screen data and maintaining the R package[synergyfinder](https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/synergyfinder.html). I just started a project in the single cell domain.
  - Attachment (Bioconductor): [synergyfinder](https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/synergyfinder.html)
> Efficient implementations for all the popular synergy scoring models for drug combinations, including HSA, Loewe, Bliss and ZIP and visualization of the synergy scores as either a two-dimensional or a three-dimensional interaction surface over the dose matrix.

**brian capaldo** (10:46:54):
> My entire grad school focused on synergy and now I find out about this package????

**Shuyu Zheng** (11:37:02) (in thread):
> It seems that we should have built it earlier.:smile:Anyway, welcome to try and comment it.

**Ruben Dries** (16:07:27):
> Hi all, I'm currently a postdoc at DFCI/Harvard in GC Yuan's lab and I will soon (August 1st) open the doors of my own lab at BUMC. I'm specifically interested in understanding developmental and cancer dynamics. Therefore I like to combine R data analysis with experimental approaches and state-of-the-art of functional genomics, like TT-seq, polyA-seq, HiChIP, ... . In addition, I also have a particular interest in single-cell and spatial transcriptomic analysis and visualization. I look forward to contribute to the Bioc Community in the future!

**Jim Hunter** (14:01:53):
> @Jim Hunter has joined the channel

**Jelmer Poelstra** (15:13:36):
> @Jelmer Poelstra has joined the channel

**Atul Deshpande** (17:06:24):
> @Atul Deshpande has joined the channel

**Atul Deshpande** (17:32:15):
> Hi all, I am a postdoc at Johns Hopkins in the Fertig Lab. I am interested in understanding intercellular interactions within the TME, hopefully through spatial omics data. As a red-green colorblind person, and given that visualizations are a big part of research communication in this field, I am also extremely interested in helping the drive to make default visualizations colorblind friendly. To illustrate my point, in the Ishihara test here, normal vision people see the number 74, and I (and a lot of colorblind people) see the number 21. With this kind of disparity in pattern recognition when relying on solely colors, it is not only important to me to have access to colorblind friendly palettes while doing my own analyses, but also to promote colorblind friendly practices in the community so that more and more research is accessible to colorblind people. I look forward to brainstorm on this as well as contribute to other bioc community initiatives in the future.
  - File (PNG): [Ishihara_9.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F015JSCC45C/ishihara_9.png)

**Simina Boca** (18:38:39):
> At least 5% of the population is color-blind. When I was in grad school (also at Hopkins!) there were at least 2 people in the genomics group who were colorblind, which meant they couldn't tell apart the "standard" red/green heatmaps.

**Dan Bunis** (18:47:46) (in thread):
> I don't even see the 21! I guess we have a different form of red-green colorblindness.

**Atul Deshpande** (18:48:27) (in thread):
> I keep switching between 21 and 81 depending on my mood.:slightly_smiling_face:

## 2020-07-19

**Kirill Tsyganov** (18:54:53):
> @Kirill Tsyganov has joined the channel

**Kirill Tsyganov** (19:30:26):
> Hey people, thanks to@Aedin Culhanefor introducing me to this community - I had no idea such cool place existed. I'm from down under (Melbourne, Australia) with Russian root:smile:. I work at the Monash University, at the Monash Bioinformatics Platform. It's too long to explain what the platform is about, but in the nutshell we are slightly bigger than just a "service providers".  Apart from working with researchers on solving biological problems with the data, we also do a hole lot of community engagement and outreach.
> Myself - I essentially work with any kind of genomics data, anything that comes of sequencing machines. I'm trying to focus more on the "secondary" type of analysis, where raw data had been transform into some sort of tabular format that fits in R so that I can start answering biological questions.
> 
> p.s I can be a little slow to respond (work load etc) please don't hold against me, I'll always answer given a little time. cheers !

**Ruth Schmidt** (10:45:03):
> Hello everyone:wave:excited to meet you all virtually! My name is[Ruth](https://ruthlydiaschmidt.com/)and I’m a microbial ecologist and data scientist in the making. Currently, I am a postdoc between INRS (National Institute of Scientific Research) and the data analytic and dataviz company Plotly in Montreal, Canada. I’m developing Dash apps in R for omics data in microbiome research and this is my first time presenting at BioC. Looking forward to sharing how to build dash apps and showing some examples:hugging_face:I’m also looking for opportunities after my postdoc. Happy to connect and chat about projects & opportunities!
  - File (GIF): [hello_bioc.gif](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F017GH3C75L/hello_bioc.gif)

**mirna** (14:34:32) (in thread):
> Hello Ruth, I was wondering if you know any metagenome binning package in R?

**Rajiv Kumar Tripathi** (14:52:04):
> @Rajiv Kumar Tripathi has joined the channel

**dnusskern** (14:59:20):
> @dnusskern has joined the channel

**Krutika** (16:03:20) (in thread):
> Hi Nur-Taz! We developed annoFuse because we saw a need for a fusion filtering and annotation  framework that is not currently available as a tool/package.  We provide users the ability to filter fusion by read support/read-throughs or if they are present in normal databases as well as annotate fusion calls as found in TCGA and gene partners as Oncogene/TSG/Kinase etc . In addition we provide summary plots and domain level plots for visualization and review.

**Ruth Schmidt** (19:18:06) (in thread):
> hey mirna! haven’t used it myself but this one seems like something you might wanna check out:[gbtools](https://github.com/kbseah/genome-bin-tools)

> Hi everyone! My name is[Mirko](https://mirkosignorelli.wixsite.com/home/)and I am a biostatistician who has been programming in R for a while now. I work as postdoc at the Leiden University Medical Center (in:flag-nl:) and my research deals with methods for the analysis of biomedical and omic data. This is my first Bioc conference and I am really enjoying it! I'm glad to have finally found out that there is such an active community behind Bioconductor:slightly_smiling_face:For full disclosure: so far I have uploadedmy[two R packages](https://mirkosignorelli.wixsite.com/home/software)to CRAN, but I am seriously considering uploading the next ones to Bioconductor!

**Riyue Sunny Bao** (09:52:25):
> Hello everyone!! I am Riyue Bao (Sunny) from Pittsburgh, I am co-directing a team of bioinformatics scientists in the UPMC Hillman Cancer Center. My group supports the translational research at the Cancer Center and we also collaborate with UPMC Children's Hospital and U Pitt. I also have my own lab focusing on tumor resistance mechanisms in immunotherapy in Dept of Medicine. We are interested in developing new methods to integrate multi-omics data, imaging data, and clinical data to find new targets that can be used to guide clinical trial design and development. - also we are actively recruiting talents to join us!! DM me, find me on twitter @RiyueSunnyBao, or shoot me an email[baor@upmc.edu](mailto:baor@upmc.edu). Open for coffee chat!!!!:slightly_smiling_face:

**Andreas Gschwind** (13:05:12):
> Hi, I’m a computational biology postdoc at Stanford University. I mostly work on single-cell sequencing data, in particular Perturb-seq type screens for functional genomics elements. Previously I worked in human and population genetics, where I investigated expression and chromatin quantitative trait loci. Currently I’m interested in developing computational methods for novel experimental approaches and making them available to the community. My first R-package TAPseq (shameless plug, sorry ;)) was accepted to Bioconductor this cycle, so I would still consider myself a Bioconductor novice. I’m really enjoying the BioC2020 conference, thanks for organizing such a great meeting!

> Wow Bioconductor has a slack channel with awesome community discussion?!  Way to go.  Zach DeBruine here from the Van Andel Institute, Michigan.   - Attachment (zachdebruine.com): [Zach DeBruine](https://www.zachdebruine.com/)

**Helge Hecht** (13:14:48):
> Hi all! I'm developing mass spectrometry pipelines in R and python and would like to learn more about Bioconductor and eventually move our packages there or make them compliant. We currently publish things in bioconda, but interfacing with the Bioconductor ecosystem would be nice!
> 
> I'd also be interested in getting involved in the review processes after publishing the first packages.

## 2021-12-07

**Castro Mbithi** (02:28:51):
> @Castro Mbithi has joined the channel

**Castro Mbithi** (02:40:08):
> Hi, I am Castro, and I look forward to learning a lot from this group. I’m a developer at[LD Talent](http://ldtalent.org), a project born out of Stanford University. It's a tool that helps you find diverse vetted developers and helps you manage them in an efficient and transparent way.***Free Trial Details: ***The platform gives you[~5 free hours](http://ldtalent.org/#pricing)to try them before hiring them for[contract or full-time](http://ldtalent.org/#pricing)roles. To see how incredibly unique LD is, check out these video clips:
> * [~1m] an[overview](https://www.youtube.com/embed/iuEKqJbm9O8?start=0&end=70)of the platform and vetting
> * [~30s][transparent work sessions](https://www.youtube.com/embed/iuEKqJbm9O8?start=832&end=865)
> * [~1m][data on developers](https://www.youtube.com/embed/iuEKqJbm9O8?start=185&end=285), i.e. productivity and responsiveness
> * [~3m][interviewing tips and time estimation](https://youtu.be/Ize-Gxaalgw?t=113)
> * [~1m][budget control](https://www.youtube.com/embed/iuEKqJbm9O8?start=924&end=1007)
> ***Diversity meets Capitalism: ***Our mission is to help teams become stronger by encouraging them to drop[location biases and constraints](https://blog.learningdollars.com/2019/11/17/how-to-end-world-poverty-and-racial-power-imbalance-in-1-generation/)that unknowingly and implicitly exclude qualified, motivated, and[efficient](https://blog.learningdollars.com/2020/03/31/developer-time-tracking-using-slack-beyond-fixed-price-vs-hourly/)people of color from impactful roles they are perfect for. These knowledge workers are undervalued and overlooked by industry, creating an opportunity for a new type of global collaboration, given the rise of[remote distributed teams](https://blog.learningdollars.com/2020/04/17/100-remote-startup-mantras/)in the new normal.
> 
> We are always looking for BRUTALLY HONEST feedback :)
  - File (GIF): [aldgif-2FPS.gif](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F02PS2TPF4J/aldgif-2fps.gif)

**Garth Kong** (20:27:52):
> Hi all, my name is Garth and I am a computational biologist at Oregon Health and Science University! I use bioinformatics to study epigenetic changes in blood cancer. I'm looking to submit my first R package through Bioconductor in the second cycle (Oct 2022), and I look forward to signing up for the New Developer Program!
> 
> Here is a link to my personal website and my lab:slightly_smiling_face:[https://gartician.github.io/](https://gartician.github.io/)[https://www.maxsonlab.org/](https://www.maxsonlab.org/)
  - Attachment (gartician.github.io): [Garth Kong Personal Website](https://gartician.github.io/)
> Your friendly research institute bioinformatic analyst
  - Attachment (maxsonlab.org): [The Maxson Laboratory](https://www.maxsonlab.org/)
> The Maxson Lab. Studying the genomics of leukemia at the OHSU Knight Cancer Institute.

**Jennifer Holmes** (16:13:47):
> Hi everyone! I'm Jennifer - You can call me Jenn. I work for the CDC Foundation and I'm stationed at the Ohio Department of Health as a Bioinformatician. I'm excited to be in the community and continue to improve my skills!:slightly_smiling_face:

**Jennifer Foltz** (10:50:15):
> Hello all! I am an Instructor at Washington University in St. Louis! Background as a wet-lab immunologist, and for the last 3+ years doing single-cell analysis:computer:on samples from immunotherapy clinical trials! Happy to be here:nerd_face:

**Matti Ruuskanen** (11:20:48):
> Hello! I'm a postdoctoral researcher in@Leo Lahti’s group at University of Turku. Mainly working on gut and skin microbiomes, but I'm also interested in environmental microbiology. Method-wise, I'm dealing with multiomic data integration, machine learning, and spatial modeling. I've worked mostly with R since 2013, dabbled in python and now  getting started with Julia. In my spare time I play music, sing in a choir and do various sports. Send me a message if you want to connect!

**Madelyn Carlson** (10:50:35) (in thread):
> Amazing and welcome Sweta! Great to e-meet you. If you haven’t done so already, you can join the#bugsigdbSlack channel to learn more about the Microbiome Study Curation project. You can also use that channel to ask questions about the project and its contribution tasks. In case it’s useful, here is the link to the[BugSigDB-Outreachy contribution guidance](https://github.com/waldronlab/BugSigDBcuration/issues/1)issues pg (this can be found pinned in the#bugsigdbchannel as well)
  - Attachment: [#1 Microbiome Study Curation - Outreachy Applicant Instructions](https://github.com/waldronlab/BugSigDBcuration/issues/1)
> Welcome Outreachy Applicants!
> 
> *Note: This task can be completed by multiple Outreachy applicants.*
> 
> **BugSigDB Project**
> 
> BugSigDB is a wiki built to capture standardized microbial signatures of health outcomes and enables the systematic comparison and synthesis of published human microbiome studies, [https://bugsigdb.org/Main_Page](https://bugsigdb.org/Main_Page). As context, the human microbiome is the collection of microorganisms colonizing the human body. It performs essential functions such as producing vitamins and important nutrients, metabolizing food and drugs and toxins, regulating epithelial development, and influencing innate immunity. It is modified by diet, medical treatment, and host-to-host transmission, and can be a mediator between environment and health. The main result of most published human microbiome studies are lists or “signatures” of differentially abundant microbial taxa in different groups, which are often classified by health condition, behavior or risk factor. A microbial signature is a list of bacteria, viruses, and/or archaea reported to be differentially abundant with respect to health outcomes (e.g., cancer, diabetes, obesity) or with respect to sociodemographic characteristics (e.g., age, smoking, income, or place of residence). These signatures are often reported in heterogeneous ways with different taxonomic nomenclature databases and do not allow direct comparison of methods or results in different studies. BugSigDB contributors aim to create a comprehensive coverage of the human microbiome literature that allows for (1) comparisons between new research findings and previously published results in any area of the health literature and (2) identify common patterns of differentially abundant microbes across otherwise disparate health outcomes.
> 
> **Prep Work**
> 
> To make a contribution to the BugSigDB project, applicants should first review the onboarding materials:  
> BugSigDB project information, estimated time: 15 minutes ([link](https://bugsigdb.org/Project:About))  
> Reading materials on the human microbiome, estimated time: 2 hours ([link](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1cpY8RoOHjJ_oxkDRGwlpqL-I29IN9B5BifhZIlqTjhE/edit))  
> Step-by-step guidance on how to document a signature in BugSigDB ([link](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yfpnwxefLFc&list=PLIUETfW6tVu2OGgZcIxqI7DbUBReHVdIq&index=5&ab_channel=WaldronLab))  
> View examples of completed BugSigDB curations, estimated time: 15 minutes ([link](https://bugsigdb.org/Study_600))  
> Video trainings on BugSigDB curation ([link](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLIUETfW6tVu2OGgZcIxqI7DbUBReHVdIq))
> 
> **Contribution Task**
> 
> Review article on the human microbiome that is heavily annotated by BugSigDB project leaders (e.g., explaining the section(s) of the article that convey study design and the specific characteristics that determine study design) and complete a mini-curation, akin to BugSigDB curations, on Qualtrics XM. Link to curation form: [https://cunysph.az1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_0cYerh299Ftl49M](https://cunysph.az1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_0cYerh299Ftl49M).
> 
> **(Optional) Stretch Task:** As an optional, higher-difficulty-level task, you may claim an article on the human microbiome from the BugSigDBcuration Github issues tagged ["paper to curate"](https://github.com/waldronlab/BugSigDBcuration/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aopen+label%3A%22paper+to+curate%22) and work on a full curation.
> 
> To claim an article for curation, post a response to the issue with your name and we will mark it as claimed.
> 
> To curate the article you select, first create a BugSigDB account ([https://bugsigdb.org/Special:RequestAccount](https://bugsigdb.org/Special:RequestAccount)) and follow the website prompts to complete the curation.
> 
> **Questions**
> 
> Communications regarding specific tasks should be done on the Slack BioC channel, to join, view [https://slack.bioconductor.org/](https://slack.bioconductor.org/) then search for the channel [#bugsigdb](https://waldronlab.slack.com/archives/C016AL6G2R0)
> 
> **Contributions**
> 
> Remember to record your contributions in Outreachy at <https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-may-2023-internship-cohort/communities/bioconductor/#microbiome-study-curation|https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-may-2023-internship-cohort/communities/bioconductor/#microbiome-study-curation>.

**Monalika Patnaik** (02:32:18) (in thread):
> Hey@MaryJones:wave:For first contribution we need to understand the basics of R and R Markdown..you can work on this issue as your first contribution task:blush:[https://github.com/Bioconductor/sweave2rmd/issues/62](https://github.com/Bioconductor/sweave2rmd/issues/62)
  - Attachment: [#62 Sweave2Rmd Knowledge Check](https://github.com/Bioconductor/sweave2rmd/issues/62)
> **Welcome Outreachy applicants!**
> 
> *Note: Multiple Outreachy applicants can work on this issue.*
> 
> Please choose this as your first contribution task to the Bioconductor Sweave2Rmd project as it will familiarize you with the R, R Markdown, GitHub processes, documentation, and the Sweave2Rmd conversion process (learn more about Bioconductor [here](https://bioconductor.org/)).
> 
> **Sweave2Rmd Project**
> 
> Sweave2Rmd is a community project to convert Sweave vignettes to Rmd. Currently, there are approximately 900 vignettes .Rnw in software packages. These vignettes do not operate as effectively as .Rmd as they are older, combine latex with R, and compile to PDF whereas .Rmd vignettes compile to HTML, are BiocStyle compatible, and easier to read and maintain. Therefore, the aim of the Sweave2Rmd project is to convert these Sweave vignettes to .Rmd.
> 
> **Prep Work**
> 
> If you're not familiar with R and R Markdown, please learn a little about them. The following links should be sufficient to get you started: [R Primer - Programming basics](https://rstudio.cloud/learn/primers/1.2) or [R Markdown](https://github.com/rstudio/cheatsheets/raw/main/rmarkdown-2.0.pdf). Then you may complete the following steps:
> 
> • Install R
> • Install RStudio
> • In RStudio or through the terminal, install devtools and BiocManager:  
>     `install.packages(c("devtools", "BiocManager"))`
> • Read through the [Sweave2Rmd Contribute article](https://bioconductor.github.io/sweave2rmd/articles/contribute.html), which explains step-by-step the process of converting a Sweave document to Rmd. The article will be important for completing this contribution task.
> 
> **Contribution Task**
> 
> After reading the Sweave2Rmd Contribute article, you may complete the [2023 Outreachy Sweave2Rmd Contribution Familiarity Quiz](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSeoN0ntSD7A33JBCzKZyMm6ZXccKQfNkemTv_8eqWkeUT7kUg/viewform?usp=sf_link). The quiz contains questions about the process of converting a Sweave vignette to Rmd. It is an opportunity to both test your familiarity with the Contribute article and learn how to convert a vignette if you choose to complete the second (optional) contribution task. We expect the quiz to take up to 3 hours to complete. If you have any questions about the quiz, feel free to post on the Sweave2Rmd Outreachy Slack channel. To join the Slack channel, view [https://slack.bioconductor.org/](https://slack.bioconductor.org/) then search for the channel #outreachy.
> 
> Remember to record your contributions in Outreachy at [https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-may-2023-internship-cohort/communities/bioconductor/convert-sweave-vignettes-to-r-markdown/contributions/](https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-may-2023-internship-cohort/communities/bioconductor/convert-sweave-vignettes-to-r-markdown/contributions/)

> Outreachy applicants:
> 
> As a reminder, we are hosting the Sweave2Rmd-Outreachy office hour at 11-11:45AM ET today ([Zoom link](https://us02web.zoom.us/j/7142141473)). This will be time where we explain the project and answer questions about the contribution tasks. If you’re unable to make it, no worries - we have another office hour session next week on Thursday, March 16, 10-10:45AM ET (and sessions each week for the 2 weeks after) and I will try my best to remember to record the session today and post it on this channel. Talk soon!

**Emmanuel Taiwo devbird007** (12:32:56):
> Hello fellow Outreachy applicants, there are a couple of channels reserved for Outreachy Contributions so you'll need to join to get updates and more information on how to contribute:
> 1. #outreachyis the general outreachy channel
> 2. #sweave2rmdis for the "[Convert Sweave Vignettes to R Markdown](https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-may-2023-internship-cohort/communities/bioconductor/#convert-sweave-vignettes-to-r-markdown)" project.
> 3. #bugsigdbis for the "[Microbiome Study Curation](https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-may-2023-internship-cohort/communities/bioconductor/#microbiome-study-curation)" project.
> 4. #miaverseis for the "[Optimize microbiome data science framework](https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-may-2023-internship-cohort/communities/bioconductor/#optimize-microbiome-data-science-framework)" project
> I hope this has been helpful. :-)

**Andres Wokaty** (13:29:23) (in thread):
> Thanks for sharing this. The third channel is#miaversefor the "[Optimize microbiome data science framework](https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-may-2023-internship-cohort/communities/bioconductor/#optimize-microbiome-data-science-framework)" project.

**Emmanuel Taiwo devbird007** (14:24:16) (in thread):
> Noted...I'll edit my message to include it.

## 2023-06-01

**Louis Le Nézet** (03:17:34):
> @Louis Le Nézet has joined the channel

**Louis Le Nézet** (03:28:09):
> Hi everyone !
> My name is Louis Le Nézet and I'm working in genetic. I've never contribute before to Bioconductor but I motivated to do so.
> I have one package that was on Cran, that could be of interest on Bioconductor (kinship2: pedigree functions). I'm also part of a group where we develop shiny modules that we would like to share as a bioconductor package.
> What would be the best course of action ?

**Lori Shepherd** (07:30:02) (in thread):
> There are a few difference from CRAN to Bioconductor  --[http://contributions.bioconductor.org/](http://contributions.bioconductor.org/)has a full run down but careful attention to the first few sections on "General BIoconductor Package Development" and "Important Bioconductor Package Development Features"   --@Aedin Culhaneis there going to be another round of the mentorship program as that might also be a good experience if you already have the basics of packages set to go

**Louis Le Nézet** (08:29:58) (in thread):
> I would be really interested in a mentorship program.
> I have the some basics knowledge on packages but I would really appreciate some guidance.

> @Esther Camacho has joined the channel

**Esther Camacho** (01:53:32):
> Hi everyone! My name is Esther and I work as a bioinformatics technician at VHIR in Barcelona. I believe this conference will be a great opportunity to broaden my knowledge and to do net-working. Nice to meet you!

**Madan Mohan** (02:29:37):
> @Madan Mohan has joined the channel

**Kacper Żukowski** (08:04:50):
> @Kacper Żukowski has joined the channel

**Mohamed S.AboHoussien** (11:02:49):
> @Mohamed S.AboHoussien has joined the channel

**Mohamed S.AboHoussien** (11:35:35):
> Hello everyone nice to meet you all
> My name is Mohamed Said Ahmed AboHussien, and I am very interested in becoming involved in research in the Metagenomics field. I have a[BSc.in](http://BSc.in)Genetics Agriculture College Al-Azhar University
> I have taken the following courses related to Metagenomics :
> I studied
> 1. QIIME 2 Cancer Microbiome Intervention Tutorial
> 2. Fecal microbiota transplant (FMT) study
> 3.“Moving Pictures” tutorial - Multiple Interface Edition
> 4.“Atacama soil microbiome” tutorial
> 5. Parkinson’s Mouse Tutorial
> 6. Differential abundance analysis with Gneiss
> For all of the above, I used the Qiime2 command line interface (Bash)
> Galaxy pipelines and workflows like
> 1. Metagenomics data analyses
> 2. Metatranscriptomics analysis
> 3. Amplicon 16srRNA data analysis
> 4. Shotgun metagenomics
> 5. Taxonomic profiling and Visualization for Meta-genomics data.
> 6. Assembly of Metagenomics sequence data
> 
> I participated in three internships related to the Bioinformatics
> 1.Galaxy Training Network GTN Smörgåsbord 3
> 2. Hack Bio Internship
> 3.H3ABioNet Internship
> 
> I have the following related experience
> • Metatranscriptomics analysis using microbiome RNA-seq data
> • Amplicon 16srRNA data analysis and visualization
> • 16S Microbial Analysis with Mothur (short)
> • AMR Antibiotic resistance detection
> • Pathogen detection from (direct Nanopore) sequencing data using Galaxy –Foodborne Edition
> • Taxonomic profiling and Visualization for Meta-genomics data
> • Assembly of Metagenomics sequence data
> • SARS_cov2 variant analysis
> • Shotgun metagenomics reveals a wide array of antibiotic resistance genes and mobile elements in a polluted lake in India
> • Efficient generation of complete sequences of MDR-encoding plasmids by rapid assembly of MinION barcoding sequencing data
> • imported: Processed Indian, Sweden Datasets: Taxonomic classification and extraction of functional information

**Yeji Bae** (18:05:00):
> @Yeji Bae has joined the channel

**Kwangwoon (Jon) Lee** (18:39:27):
> @Kwangwoon (Jon) Lee has joined the channel

## 2023-08-28

**Mikaila Chetty** (07:42:43):
> Hi all! I'm Mikaila and work as a researcher/PhD candidate. I work on C. elegan model organism (everything from locomotion, behavior, oxidative stress, mitochondria assays, protein assays, transriptomics and molecular docking are all my kind of thing). My long term goals are to focus on Aging & Alzheimer's in the biotech/pharma industry. I am looking forward to connecting with everyone. Also - all guidance and tips on the industry & career development are welcome :)
> 
> Feel free to connect with me here:
> • Twitter ([https://twitter.com/mikaila_chetty_](https://twitter.com/mikaila_chetty_))
> • LinkedIn ([https://www.linkedin.com/in/mikailachetty/](https://www.linkedin.com/in/mikailachetty/))

## 2023-10-06

**Dimitri Meistermann** (01:33:10) (in thread):
> My first submission, a package named "markergrid", is close to be ready. I just have a slight conflict between biocheck and roxygen that creates a file named  R/markergrid-package.R and man/markergrid-package.Rd. As I understand those are created to manage namespace and create a global manpage to the package. My problem is that biocheck require that 80% of manpages should have a runnable example, and as the package is very small I have only one manpage for my function, so only 50% of .Rd files have examples. But man/markergrid-package.Rd doesn't contain any function so it doesn't make sense to me to include runnable examples.
> 
> I'm not sure that introductions thread is the right place to ask, so I can move my question elsewhere.

**Lluís Revilla** (03:47:17) (in thread):
> The namespace of the package is controlled by the file NAMESPACE. It is based on the`#' @export`tags you use.
> Yes, usually help pages for the package do not include examples. This can be explained to the reviewer and I'm sure they will understand. However, it is not normal for a package to have just a single function.

## 2023-10-07

**Hodan Abdirisak** (00:30:14) (in thread):
> Warm welcoming,@Sophie Sorensonyour contribution will be easy and smoothly follow this where you get instructions about how to make first contribution[https://github.com/waldronlab/BugSigDBcuration/issues/94](https://github.com/waldronlab/BugSigDBcuration/issues/94)and you can ask help to us every time you need
  - Attachment: [#94  Welcome Outreachy Applicants!](https://github.com/waldronlab/BugSigDBcuration/issues/94)
> **Welcome Outreachy Applicants!**
> 
> Note: This task can be completed by multiple Outreachy applicants.
> 
> **BugSigDB Project**
> 
> BugSigDB is a wiki built to capture standardized microbial signatures of health outcomes and enables the systematic comparison and synthesis of published human microbiome studies, [https://bugsigdb.org/Main_Page](https://bugsigdb.org/Main_Page). As context, the human microbiome is the collection of microorganisms colonizing the human body. It performs essential functions such as producing vitamins and important nutrients, metabolizing food and drugs and toxins, regulating epithelial development, and influencing innate immunity. It is modified by diet, medical treatment, and host-to-host transmission, and can be a mediator between environment and health. The main result of most published human microbiome studies are lists or “signatures” of differentially abundant microbial taxa in different groups, which are often classified by health condition, behavior or risk factor. A microbial signature is a list of bacteria, viruses, and/or archaea reported to be differentially abundant with respect to health outcomes (e.g., cancer, diabetes, obesity) or with respect to sociodemographic characteristics (e.g., age, smoking, income, or place of residence). These signatures are often reported in heterogeneous ways with different taxonomic nomenclature databases and do not allow direct comparison of methods or results in different studies. BugSigDB contributors aim to create a comprehensive coverage of the human microbiome literature that allows for (1) comparisons between new research findings and previously published results in any area of the health literature and (2) identify common patterns of differentially abundant microbes across otherwise disparate health outcomes.
> 
> **Prep Work**
> 
> To make a contribution to the BugSigDB project, applicants should first review the onboarding materials:  
> BugSigDB project information, estimated time: 15 minutes ([link](https://bugsigdb.org/Project:About))  
> Reading materials on the human microbiome, estimated time: 2 hours ([link](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1cpY8RoOHjJ_oxkDRGwlpqL-I29IN9B5BifhZIlqTjhE/edit))  
> Step-by-step guidance on how to document a signature in BugSigDB ([link](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yfpnwxefLFc&list=PLIUETfW6tVu2OGgZcIxqI7DbUBReHVdIq&index=5&ab_channel=WaldronLab))  
> View examples of completed BugSigDB curations, estimated time: 15 minutes ([link](https://bugsigdb.org/Study_600))  
> Video trainings on BugSigDB curation ([link](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLIUETfW6tVu2OGgZcIxqI7DbUBReHVdIq))
> 
> **First Contribution Task**
> 
> 1. Go to [https://cunysph.az1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_7UTYhqDc3qYwiEu](https://cunysph.az1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_7UTYhqDc3qYwiEu)
> 2. Review the article on the human microbiome that has been heavily annotated by BugSigDB project leaders (e.g., explaining the section(s) of the article that convey study design and the specific characteristics that determine study design)
> 3. Complete a mini-curation, akin to BugSigDB curations, on Qualtrics XM.
> 
> Link to curation form: [https://cunysph.az1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_7UTYhqDc3qYwiEu](https://cunysph.az1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_7UTYhqDc3qYwiEu)
> 
> **Please submit a fully completed mini-curation via the form above before continuing to the second contribution task.**
> 
> **Second Contribution Task (Optional)**
> 
> As an optional, higher-difficulty-level task, you may claim an article on the human microbiome from the BugSigDBcuration Github issues tagged ["paper to curate"](https://github.com/waldronlab/BugSigDBcuration/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aopen+label%3A%22paper+to+curate%22) and work on a full curation.
> 
> To claim an article for curation, post a response to the issue with your name and we will mark it as claimed. Please only claim one article for curation until you have completed this curation and it has been graded.
> 
> To curate the article you select, first create a BugSigDB account ([https://bugsigdb.org/Special:RequestAccount](https://bugsigdb.org/Special:RequestAccount)) and follow the website prompts to complete the curation.
> 
> **Assessment & Grading**
> 
> Your first contribution will be scored for accuracy. Your second contribution will be scored with the following rubric:
> 
> 1. All elements marked "Needs review" (none "Incomplete") (1 point):
> 2. Correct study design (1 point):
> 3. Entered all relevant experiments and no irrelevant experiments (1 point):
> 4. Body site correctly identified (i.e. does not include multiple sites) (1 point):
> 5. Condition entered according to contrast (correct EFO ontology) (1 point):
> 6. Contrast groups correctly identified (1 point):
> 7. Groups correctly labeled as 1 and 0 (1=cases, 0=controls) (1 point):
> 8. Antibiotic exclusion correctly identified (1 point):
> 9. Correctly identified sequencing details (1 point for sequencing type and variable region, 1 point for sequencing platform) (2 points):
> 10. Identified correct statistical test (1 point):
> 11. Identified MHT correction (1 point):
> 12. Correctly recorded matched on variables (1 point):
> 13. Entered correct number of statistical tests per experiment (1 point):
> 14. All diversity measures identified (1 point):
> 15. Diversity results correctly entered as increased/ decreased/ unchanged (1 point):
> 16. All signature sources correctly identified (-1 for each error) (2 points):
> 17. Abundance direction correctly selected (1 point):
> 18. Members of signatures identified correctly (1 point for single small error, 0 points for anything more. Incorrect means missing or extraneous taxon) (2 points):
> 19. Correct use of NCBI taxonomy (don't deduct if can't easily find correct taxon in NCBI taxonomy database. 1 for one error, 0 for multiple errors) (2 points):
> 
> Total (maximum 24 points):
> 
> Please note it may take us several days to score your second contribution, depending on how many contributions we receive. Once scored, you will have the opportunity to revise your curation to remedy any identified errors; however, we will record both your original and revised score when evaluating your second contribution. We strongly recommend you ask questions on our Slack channel, compare your curation to completed curations on BugSigDB, and attend office hours prior to submitting your second contribution for grading.
> 
> **Questions**
> 
> Communications regarding specific tasks should be done on the Slack BioC channel, to join, view [https://slack.bioconductor.org/](https://slack.bioconductor.org/) then search for the channel [#bugsigdb](https://waldronlab.slack.com/archives/C016AL6G2R0)
> 
> To reduce duplicate questions, please do not directly message or email the BugSigDB mentors.
> 
> **Office Hours**
> 
> Office hours will be held at 10 AM EDT (eastern daylight time) every Thursday at: [https://us02web.zoom.us/j/2737200499](https://us02web.zoom.us/j/2737200499). Attendance by all applicants is highly encouraged. Find out the time in your local time zone here: [https://www.timeanddate.com/worldclock/converter.html?iso=20230929T140000&p1=179](https://www.timeanddate.com/worldclock/converter.html?iso=20230929T140000&p1=179)
> 
> **Contributions**
> 
> Remember to record your contributions in Outreachy at [https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-december-2023-internship-round/communities/bioconductor/microbiome-study-curation](https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-december-2023-internship-round/communities/bioconductor/microbiome-study-curation).

## 2023-10-08

**Odigiri Great Alume** (02:17:09):
> Good morning guys. 
> So I need clarification on something please:pleading_face:I'm working on "Lower Respiratory Tract microbiome composition and smokers behavior"  
> 
> The study subjects were grouped into three: Never smokers (NS), Active Smokers (AS) and Former smokers (FS). 
> 
> The study employed the Prospective cohort study design.
> 
> Please how do I place these subjects into GROUP 1 and GROUP 0 seeing that they are three groups (NF, AF, FS)?:thinking_face:

**Odigiri Great Alume** (02:19:08):
  - File (JPEG): [Screenshot_20231007-005253.jpg](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F060WH8GSQ0/screenshot_20231007-005253.jpg)

**Chioma Onyido** (02:37:41) (in thread):
> Hi@Odigiri Great AlumeI think one thing is to check the groups that were contrasted during the differential abundance analysis of the microbial taxa.

**Chioma Onyido** (02:44:11) (in thread):
> Group 0 is usually the control/ reference group, while Group 1 is the “case”, for example, if the study is about AD, Group 0 is the normal group, I.e those without AD, while Group 1 would be those with AD.
> 
> But sometimes it could get tricky. 
> In your case, maybe differential abundance was reported for NS vs AS and FS vs AS.
> In this case you might have to create separate experiments for both. A clue is to check the figures, focus more on the table/ figure where the differential abundant taxa was reported, that should give you an idea.
> 
> I hope this helps.

**Odigiri Great Alume** (04:00:11) (in thread):
> Thanks dear. I will look now

> @Tim Treis has left the channel

**Odigiri Great Alume** (14:49:52) (in thread):
> Alright@Andres WokatyI will

**Mildred Anashie** (14:42:49) (in thread):
> Also[https://github.com/waldronlab/BugSigDBcuration/issues/94](https://github.com/waldronlab/BugSigDBcuration/issues/94)This could be helpful
  - Attachment: [#94  Welcome Outreachy Applicants!](https://github.com/waldronlab/BugSigDBcuration/issues/94)
> **Welcome Outreachy Applicants!**
> 
> Note: This task can be completed by multiple Outreachy applicants.
> 
> **BugSigDB Project**
> 
> BugSigDB is a wiki built to capture standardized microbial signatures of health outcomes and enables the systematic comparison and synthesis of published human microbiome studies, [https://bugsigdb.org/Main_Page](https://bugsigdb.org/Main_Page). As context, the human microbiome is the collection of microorganisms colonizing the human body. It performs essential functions such as producing vitamins and important nutrients, metabolizing food and drugs and toxins, regulating epithelial development, and influencing innate immunity. It is modified by diet, medical treatment, and host-to-host transmission, and can be a mediator between environment and health. The main result of most published human microbiome studies are lists or “signatures” of differentially abundant microbial taxa in different groups, which are often classified by health condition, behavior or risk factor. A microbial signature is a list of bacteria, viruses, and/or archaea reported to be differentially abundant with respect to health outcomes (e.g., cancer, diabetes, obesity) or with respect to sociodemographic characteristics (e.g., age, smoking, income, or place of residence). These signatures are often reported in heterogeneous ways with different taxonomic nomenclature databases and do not allow direct comparison of methods or results in different studies. BugSigDB contributors aim to create a comprehensive coverage of the human microbiome literature that allows for (1) comparisons between new research findings and previously published results in any area of the health literature and (2) identify common patterns of differentially abundant microbes across otherwise disparate health outcomes.
> 
> **Prep Work**
> 
> To make a contribution to the BugSigDB project, applicants should first review the onboarding materials:  
> BugSigDB project information, estimated time: 15 minutes ([link](https://bugsigdb.org/Project:About))  
> Reading materials on the human microbiome, estimated time: 2 hours ([link](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1cpY8RoOHjJ_oxkDRGwlpqL-I29IN9B5BifhZIlqTjhE/edit))  
> Step-by-step guidance on how to document a signature in BugSigDB ([link](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yfpnwxefLFc&list=PLIUETfW6tVu2OGgZcIxqI7DbUBReHVdIq&index=5&ab_channel=WaldronLab))  
> View examples of completed BugSigDB curations, estimated time: 15 minutes ([link](https://bugsigdb.org/Study_600))  
> Video trainings on BugSigDB curation ([link](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLIUETfW6tVu2OGgZcIxqI7DbUBReHVdIq))
> 
> **First Contribution Task**
> 
> 1. Go to [https://cunysph.az1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_7UTYhqDc3qYwiEu](https://cunysph.az1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_7UTYhqDc3qYwiEu)
> 2. Review the article on the human microbiome that has been heavily annotated by BugSigDB project leaders (e.g., explaining the section(s) of the article that convey study design and the specific characteristics that determine study design)
> 3. Complete a mini-curation, akin to BugSigDB curations, on Qualtrics XM.
> 
> Link to curation form: [https://cunysph.az1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_7UTYhqDc3qYwiEu](https://cunysph.az1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_7UTYhqDc3qYwiEu)
> 
> **Please submit a fully completed mini-curation via the form above before continuing to the second contribution task.**
> 
> **Second Contribution Task (Optional)**
> 
> As an optional, higher-difficulty-level task, you may claim an article on the human microbiome from the BugSigDBcuration Github issues tagged ["paper to curate"](https://github.com/waldronlab/BugSigDBcuration/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aopen+label%3A%22paper+to+curate%22) and work on a full curation.
> 
> To claim an article for curation, post a response to the issue with your name and we will mark it as claimed. Please only claim one article for curation until you have completed this curation and it has been graded.
> 
> To curate the article you select, first create a BugSigDB account ([https://bugsigdb.org/Special:RequestAccount](https://bugsigdb.org/Special:RequestAccount)) and follow the website prompts to complete the curation.
> 
> **Assessment & Grading**
> 
> Your first contribution will be scored for accuracy. Your second contribution will be scored with the following rubric:
> 
> 1. All elements marked "Needs review" (none "Incomplete") (1 point):
> 2. Correct study design (1 point):
> 3. Entered all relevant experiments and no irrelevant experiments (1 point):
> 4. Body site correctly identified (i.e. does not include multiple sites) (1 point):
> 5. Condition entered according to contrast (correct EFO ontology) (1 point):
> 6. Contrast groups correctly identified (1 point):
> 7. Groups correctly labeled as 1 and 0 (1=cases, 0=controls) (1 point):
> 8. Antibiotic exclusion correctly identified (1 point):
> 9. Correctly identified sequencing details (1 point for sequencing type and variable region, 1 point for sequencing platform) (2 points):
> 10. Identified correct statistical test (1 point):
> 11. Identified MHT correction (1 point):
> 12. Correctly recorded matched on variables (1 point):
> 13. Entered correct number of statistical tests per experiment (1 point):
> 14. All diversity measures identified (1 point):
> 15. Diversity results correctly entered as increased/ decreased/ unchanged (1 point):
> 16. All signature sources correctly identified (-1 for each error) (2 points):
> 17. Abundance direction correctly selected (1 point):
> 18. Members of signatures identified correctly (1 point for single small error, 0 points for anything more. Incorrect means missing or extraneous taxon) (2 points):
> 19. Correct use of NCBI taxonomy (don't deduct if can't easily find correct taxon in NCBI taxonomy database. 1 for one error, 0 for multiple errors) (2 points):
> 
> Total (maximum 24 points):
> 
> Please note it may take us several days to score your second contribution, depending on how many contributions we receive. Once scored, you will have the opportunity to revise your curation to remedy any identified errors; however, we will record both your original and revised score when evaluating your second contribution. We strongly recommend you ask questions on our Slack channel, compare your curation to completed curations on BugSigDB, and attend office hours prior to submitting your second contribution for grading.
> 
> **Questions**
> 
> Communication regarding specific tasks should be done on a task's GitHub issue or on the Bioconductor Slack channel #bugsigdb, which is monitored by mentors and other project participants who can help you. You're also welcome to introduce yourself on the Bioconductor Slack channel #outreachy. Sign up for an account at [https://slack.bioconductor.org/](https://slack.bioconductor.org/). After you log in, join the channels #bugsigdb and #outreachy.
> 
> To reduce duplicate questions, please do not directly message or email the BugSigDB mentors.
> 
> **Office Hours**
> 
> Office hours will be held at 10 AM EDT (eastern daylight time) every Thursday at: [https://us02web.zoom.us/j/2737200499](https://us02web.zoom.us/j/2737200499). Attendance by all applicants is highly encouraged. Find out the time in your local time zone here: [https://www.timeanddate.com/worldclock/converter.html?iso=20230929T140000&p1=179](https://www.timeanddate.com/worldclock/converter.html?iso=20230929T140000&p1=179)
> 
> **Contributions**
> 
> Remember to record your contributions in Outreachy at [https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-december-2023-internship-round/communities/bioconductor/microbiome-study-curation](https://www.outreachy.org/outreachy-december-2023-internship-round/communities/bioconductor/microbiome-study-curation).

## 2023-12-29

**Manvi Yaduvanshi** (09:00:21):
> Hey everyone! This is Manvi Yaduvanshi from India. I'm new to open source and really want to contribute to Bioconductor, but it seems bit confusing in the beginning. Can anyone help me, letting me know where to start and what documentations should I read?

**Lori Shepherd** (09:14:51):
> @Manvi YaduvanshiHello and Welcome!  What is your background?  Are you interested in a particular area of research or programming?

**Manvi Yaduvanshi** (09:26:40) (in thread):
> Hey Lori! I do programming in JAVA and python along with frontend web-development and ML and is acquainted to GoLang and R as well. I read about contributing packages to Bioc but couldn't figure out how would i know if the similar package has been built before. Was a bit confused of how would i know the requirements. I honestly can work on any internal project , I'll learn side by side if not awared about the tech stack. I'm inclined towards programming and really wanted to know about any beginner projects inside Bioc where i can start contributing and familiarize myself with Bioc more. Can you please help?

**Lori Shepherd** (09:40:42) (in thread):
> Many are out with the new year holiday so it may be a few days before other offer some additional suggestions.
> 
> Information on developing packages can be found here:[http://contributions.bioconductor.org/](http://contributions.bioconductor.org/).  There has been a recent interest in trying to link better with other programming languages so you might have some novel ideas that could really be useful.
> 
> Some existing packages that try to link python like basilisk might be worth looking at as well as the packages that utilize them.[https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/basilisk.html](https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/basilisk.html).  And a dedicated#biocpythonchannel for talking about related ideas.
> 
> We are in process of moving over a new website design and looking for feedback and improvements too if your interested in that area. There is a#biocwebsitechannel for it.[http://new.bioconductor.org/](http://new.bioconductor.org/)this layout will become live January 3 at[https://bioconductor.org/](https://bioconductor.org/)but we want to continue to improve and enhance various elements.
> 
> There is also a#cloud-working-groupthat is looking at cloud related solutions for bioconductor resources.
> 
> those might be some places to start.

**Manvi Yaduvanshi** (09:56:16) (in thread):
> Thankyou so much Lori! Couldn't ask for more.I'll surely check them out:blush:

**Krithika Bhuvanesh** (23:14:42) (in thread):
> Welcome Manvi ! Do you have a particular data type of interest such as RNA-seq or anything else ? That may help you narrow down the list as well .


## 2024-01-02

**Manvi Yaduvanshi** (10:23:45) (in thread):
> Hey!@Krithika Bhuvanesh, thanks for your response. Honestly , I do not have a prior experience in Genomic Data. But definitely would love to learn and contribute. Could you suggest me any data types , a beginner like me can start with?

**Krithika Bhuvanesh** (15:14:07) (in thread):
> If you are looking to start learning about genomic data, i recommend a coursera on genomic data and technologies. There is one from Johns Hopkins. It’s a good introduction to the data type, and they also have some hands on workshops that use R and BioC packages.


## 2024-01-03

**Manvi Yaduvanshi** (06:45:16) (in thread):
> Thanks! Sure, I'll check it out.

## 2024-01-21

**Saga** (14:40:44):
> Hello everyone :))  I am sagana from India.I am new to open source. I recently created my first pull request in bioconductor as a start (check it out:[https://github.com/Bioconductor/bioconductor.org/pull/258](https://github.com/Bioconductor/bioconductor.org/pull/258)). Itwas a good experience, and I'm eager to continue contributing .I'm keen to explore new opportunities for collaboration and growth. I'm reaching out today to seek guidance on areas where my skills and passion can be of service.I'm open to contributing in various areas—be it coding, documentation, or any other aspect where I can leverage my skills.I look forward to connecting with all of you:handshake:If anyone could point me in the right direction or recommend essential documentations to read, I would greatly appreciate your assistance. Thank You!
  - Attachment: [#258 Change Low Resolution Icon](https://github.com/Bioconductor/bioconductor.org/pull/258)
> changed the low resolution image icon that present in home page - (added new svg file- fixes [#257](https://github.com/Bioconductor/bioconductor.org/issues/257))  
> [old](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/81551211/296704328-fd2aa839-4645-4d0d-af1a-57452e95c507.png?jwt=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..Lx6jGRtKtQE4tHObWxAr7_7jvHlMG96MrRnKJJBTjWU)  
> [after adding new icon](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/81551211/296704517-0511534b-5017-49e1-ac55-6934ab92a175.png?jwt=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..UfLSBP0l7Icdgb0PNemIYM5wStruve8mpMi-fo7ttgw)


## 2024-01-22

**Maria Doyle** (16:24:32) (in thread):
> Hi Sagana,
> Welcome to the Bioconductor community and congrats on your first PR!:rocket:To learn more about contributing to Bioconductor packages you could start here[https://contributions.bioconductor.org/](https://contributions.bioconductor.org/). Your coding and documentation skills will come in handy. Feel free to DM me if you need guidance or have questions.
  - Attachment (contributions.bioconductor.org): [Welcome | Bioconductor Packages: Development, Maintenance, and Peer Review](https://contributions.bioconductor.org/)

>

> **Thank you so much for your interest and we look forward to working with you.**

**Esther Nelson** (11:47:21) (in thread):
> Hi@Oluwatoyosi Victoria Olanrewaju, welcome:hugging_face:. It's great to have you. Here are a few steps to get to speed with the exciting developments on this project:
> 
> Join the#bugsigdbchannel and you can also go through this to get started.
> 
> Follow this,[https://community-bioc.slack.com/archives/C04RATV9VCY/p1709568003471699](https://community-bioc.slack.com/archives/C04RATV9VCY/p1709568003471699), to begin your first contribution.
> 
> One of our mentors@Chioma Onyidowrote an amazing blog on getting started and what's to be expected from us during this stage,[https://chiomaonyido.wordpress.com/2024/03/05/from-learner-to-contributor-strategies-for-making-the-most-out-of-your-outreachy-contribution-stage/](https://chiomaonyido.wordpress.com/2024/03/05/from-learner-to-contributor-strategies-for-making-the-most-out-of-your-outreachy-contribution-stage/). It's gold if you ask me.
> 
> These should be enough to get you started. Oh, and once you've made your first contribution, don't forget to record it. Great to connect with you. (edited)
